quantum mechanics

Black-box empirical predictions (Schrödinger equation, matrix mechanics) worked perfectly but mechanism remained opaque. Community split: instrumentalists said 'prediction is enough,' realists demanded ontological interpretation. Massive acceleration in predictive power outpaced interpretability.

Copenhagen interpretation won institutional acceptance by explicitly rejecting the demand for mechanism ('shut up and calculate'). Competing interpretations (Many-Worlds, Bohmian) remained marginalized despite logical coherence. The field moved forward without consensus on what was actually happening.
Both involve high-precision black-box systems where predictive success triggers urgent demand for mechanistic understanding, but institutional pressure favors deployment over interpretability.
thermodynamics & statistical mechanics

Macroscopic thermodynamic laws (entropy, heat flow) predicted perfectly without revealing molecular mechanism. Atomism was controversial and unobservable. Boltzmann's kinetic theory proposed mechanism but faced fierce resistance from energeticists who saw no need to 'look inside the box.'

Molecular mechanism won, but only after decades of acceleration in chemical and physical applications made the gap unbearable. Brownian motion (1905) provided empirical forcing function. The interpretability demand was vindicated, but 50+ years after initial pressure.
System predicts without explaining; acceleration creates epistemic anxiety; reductionist interpretability eventually wins, but only when external empirical pressure forces it.

Both modern LLM interpretability and these historical cases share a structural tension: exponential capability growth decoupled from mechanistic understanding triggers a paradigm crisis where instrumentalism (prediction-sufficiency) competes with realism (mechanism-necessity). The historical pattern suggests resolution occurs when: (1) capability acceleration outpaces theoretical coherence, and (2) external empirical phenomena force interpretability to become predictively necessary, not merely philosophically desirable.

When a predictive system achieves capability thresholds that exceed the explanatory reach of its constructors by more than one order of magnitude, an interpretability crisis emerges within 10-20 years, resolved only when mechanistic understanding becomes a prerequisite for further capability gains rather than a post-hoc luxury.

Exponential capability growth creates an epistemic debt: practitioners accumulate predictive success without corresponding mechanistic models, which initially feels acceptable but eventually produces catastrophic failures or theoretical dead-ends that cannot be addressed by scaling alone, forcing the community to retroactively build the mechanistic scaffolding that pure instrumentalism deferred.

Mechanistic interpretability of LLMs will transition from a niche academic pursuit to a central engineering requirement within 5-10 years, specifically when a high-stakes deployment failure occurs that cannot be diagnosed or corrected without circuit-level or feature-level understanding — at which point interpretability funding and publication rates will spike discontinuously, mirroring Boltzmann's vindication after Perrin's Brownian motion experiments (1908) or the Born rule's forced adoption after quantum measurement anomalies accumulated.

[1920s-1930s] Quantum mechanics achieved extraordinary predictive precision via Schrödinger and Heisenberg formalisms while mechanism remained philosophically contested for decades; resolution was forced not by philosophical argument but by empirical pressure from measurement theory and later quantum field theory requiring coherent ontology to extend the formalism further.

[1860s-1900s] Thermodynamic laws predicted macroscopic heat behavior with full empirical adequacy while Boltzmann's atomistic mechanism was actively resisted by Mach and the energeticists as unnecessary metaphysics; vindication came only when Perrin's 1908 Brownian motion measurements provided externally compelled empirical necessity for the molecular mechanism, triggering rapid institutional acceptance.

[2023-present] LLMs demonstrate superhuman performance on benchmarks while alignment failures, hallucinations, and unpredictable capability jumps remain unexplained by any mechanistic model; early circuit-analysis work (induction heads, superposition hypothesis, sparse autoencoders) mirrors Boltzmann's early atomism — formally coherent but institutionally marginal — suggesting the field is in the pre-Perrin phase awaiting an empirical forcing event.
